title: Nardo Loses Her Marbles

jokes.text: Two pennies, a dime and a Canadian quarter?
jokes.setup: Louie asks how much money Latka made from driving cab all day
jokes.punchline: Reveals the pathetically small amount with specific detail of worthless Canadian quarter
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Latka's innocent incompetence contrasted with the specificity of the tiny earnings. The Canadian quarter detail is perfectly observed.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: How much money did you make today?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Thirty-six dollars and seventy-five cents.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's not bad.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yeah, but twenty-five cents of that is Canadian.

jokes.text: Police state.
jokes.setup: Louie demands Latka empty all his pockets for inspection
jokes.punchline: Latka's matter-of-fact observation about the authoritarian treatment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan delivery. Latka's immigrant perspective making an astute political observation while being shaken down is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Empty your pockets. I want to see everything.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You know, in my country, we have a saying: 'When the little man with the big mouth tells you to empty your pockets, you empty your pockets.'

jokes.text: Eh, sam-a, sam-a, sam-a-vich, sam-a-vich. A sam-a-wich. No, don't eat the sam-a-mich... Get it out.
jokes.setup: Latka pulls out a sandwich and mispronounces it
jokes.punchline: Starts to eat it mid-pocket-emptying until Louie stops him
jokes.explanation: Classic Latka behavior - the mispronunciation and casual eating attempt feel authentic to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Look, I got myself a nice sandwich here.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Latka, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I'm eating my sandwich!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're eating it out of your pocket!

jokes.text: Company property.
jokes.setup: Latka pulls out a windshield wiper from his pocket
jokes.punchline: Louie's indignant claim of ownership over this random car part
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - his petty authoritarianism over even the most trivial items shows his character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey! That's my windshield wiper!

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Your windshield wiper? How is this your windshield wiper?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I don't know, but if it's here, it's mine!

jokes.text: See, this is the Louie I was telling you about.
jokes.setup: Louie angrily demands the cat be removed
jokes.punchline: Latka introduces his cat to the notorious dispatcher
jokes.explanation: Latka's innocent introduction of his cat to the tyrannical boss is perfectly absurd and in character.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: This is my cat, Simka. Simka, this is Louie De Palma. He's very mean to everybody.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But don't worry, he's not going to hurt you. He's just going to yell and scream and make your life miserable.

jokes.text: Oh, he knocked me out in the second round.
jokes.setup: Elaine asks how Tony's fight with Gomez went
jokes.punchline: Casual delivery of getting knocked out
jokes.explanation: Tony's casual attitude toward getting beaten establishes his character's sweet optimism.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh, he knocked me out in the first round.

jokes.text: Well, I went to a bad play and a short fight.
jokes.setup: Elaine asks what Alex has been up to, after hearing Tony lost a fight and Bobby's play closed
jokes.punchline: Alex's dry summary combining both disappointments into his experience
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure and economy. Alex's deadpan delivery turns two separate failures into one perfectly crafted response.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, I've been keeping busy. Tony got beaten up, and Bobby's play closed. So I've had the unique experience of watching my friends fail in two completely different ways.

jokes.text: Yeah, everyone needs to loaf once in awhile.
jokes.setup: Elaine lists all her tasks during 'relaxation'
jokes.punchline: Alex's dry observation about her definition of loafing
jokes.explanation: Nice understated reaction to Elaine's manic behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You call that relaxing?

jokes.text: From nowhere to almost somewhere.
jokes.setup: Elaine excitedly explains how the gallery exhibit could boost her career
jokes.punchline: Alex's realistic assessment of the minimal advancement possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alex line - supportive but brutally realistic about the modest nature of artistic progress.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: This could be my big break! A whole gallery exhibit of my work!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Elaine, it's a nice opportunity, but let's be realistic. You're still going to be working here at the garage.

jokes.text: I'm wearing her down.
jokes.setup: Elaine angrily pays Louie to stop his advances and calls him a creep
jokes.punchline: Louie's delusional interpretation of clear rejection as progress
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie delusion - his complete misreading of social cues is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: She called me a creep! That's a pet name. We're getting closer.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Next week, she'll probably call me a pervert. The week after that, we'll be engaged.

jokes.text: Three. Okay. I lied, I had eight. I lied, that's hot chocolate.
jokes.setup: Asked how many cups of coffee he's had
jokes.punchline: Escalating lies reveal his actual state
jokes.explanation: Nice escalating reveal structure, though not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Oh, maybe two or three cups.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Okay, fine, five or six.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Alright, I'll be honest - I've had about twelve cups.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Maybe more like twenty.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I lost count after thirty.

jokes.text: Gin.
jokes.setup: Elaine rushes off to handle gallery crisis, promising to finish the poker hand
jokes.punchline: She wins immediately with gin, catching everyone with points
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - in her frantic state, she accidentally wins the game instantly.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I have to go, there's a crisis at the gallery. Just deal me out.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Okay, we'll finish without you.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, no, just hold my hand. I'll be right back.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alright, we're holding it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Gin!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait, you haven't even left yet!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I know. I won. You all have points.

jokes.text: She should have told me that before I lined them with tin foil.
jokes.setup: Elaine warns not to stuff food in pockets like last time
jokes.punchline: Tony reveals he came prepared with foil-lined pockets
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Tony's boxing-damaged logic leading to elaborate preparation for food theft.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Don't stuff food in your pockets like last time.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I came prepared. I've got foil-lined pockets.

jokes.text: Hey, Elaine, I'll show you an artist.
jokes.setup: Elaine introduces Fran as one of their best artists
jokes.punchline: Tony's crude attempt to one-up the introduction
jokes.explanation: Weak joke relying on Tony's crude character, but lacks wit or surprise.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, well I've seen her work. It's pretty good, but nothing compared to what I can do with a little Turtle Wax and a buffer.

jokes.text: I think this one could use more brown.
jokes.setup: Artist Phillip is upset about his unfinished paintings being displayed
jokes.punchline: Tony's completely unhelpful art criticism at the worst possible moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment - Tony's well-meaning but catastrophically inappropriate art advice destroys the situation.

dialogue.speaker: Phillip
dialogue.line: Tony, these paintings aren't finished! They shouldn't be displayed yet!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? They're beautiful! You know what your problem is? You're too much of a perfectionist. These paintings have real energy, real passion. You should be proud of them.

dialogue.speaker: Phillip
dialogue.line: Tony, you don't understand art—

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Don't understand art? I know art. My mother had a picture of dogs playing poker in her living room for thirty years. Now that's art.

jokes.text: You want bubbles? [BLOWS IN CHAMPAGNE]
jokes.setup: Gallery owner complains about flat champagne lacking bubbles
jokes.punchline: Alex literally blows bubbles into the champagne
jokes.explanation: Brilliant physical comedy - literal solution to abstract complaint. Great visual gag.

dialogue.speaker: Gallery Owner
dialogue.line: This champagne is flat. Where are the bubbles?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No problem.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Here you go.

jokes.text: You want bubbles?
jokes.setup: Gallery owner complains about flat champagne lacking bubbles
jokes.punchline: Alex creates bubbles by blowing into the champagne glass
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup, though the execution is more crude than clever.

dialogue.speaker: Gallery Owner
dialogue.line: This champagne is flat. There are no bubbles.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No problem.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Here, let me help you with that.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: There. Now you've got bubbles.

jokes.text: I guess that's what I should expect from a cabdriver.
jokes.setup: Alex's champagne bubble solution
jokes.punchline: Snooty dismissal sets up Alex's response
jokes.explanation: Sets up Alex's retaliation but isn't particularly clever on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Gallery Owner
dialogue.line: This is bubble solution? It looks like champagne.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It is champagne. I'm an optimist.

jokes.text: Low bridge!
jokes.setup: Gallery owner insults Alex as 'just a cabdriver'
jokes.punchline: Alex warns of low bridge before punching the snob
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the setup. Alex's cab driver warning before the punch is perfectly crafted and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Watch your head.

dialogue.speaker: Gallery Owner
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: There's a low bridge ahead.

jokes.text: I can tell a manic-depressive, Down syndrome when I see one.
jokes.setup: Alex is trying to convince Elaine she needs psychiatric help
jokes.punchline: His confident but completely wrong psychological diagnosis
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex's well-meaning but ignorant attempt at psychology. The medical terminology confusion is mildly amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Elaine, I've been observing you, and I think you need to see a psychiatrist.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What? Why would I need a psychiatrist?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, it's clear to me that you're suffering from a severe case of... reverse paranoia.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Reverse paranoia? What is that?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You think everyone is out to help you.

jokes.text: The day you were that red and white striped dress.
jokes.setup: Elaine asks when getting closer has crossed Alex's mind
jokes.punchline: Alex's specific, honest memory of one particular outfit
jokes.explanation: Beautifully written moment - Alex's specific honesty about attraction is both funny and touching.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, there was this one time you wore that yellow dress with the little flowers on it, and you bent over to pick up a file, and I thought, 'Boy, I could really go for a tuna sandwich right about now.'

jokes.text: Very little. I painted it.
jokes.setup: Elaine honestly says his painting stinks and asks if he paid much for it
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's the artist, making her criticism hilariously awkward
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/payoff structure creating awkward comedy from Elaine's brutal honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: I painted it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: I sometimes use the painting to test my patients' honesty. Boy, are you honest.
jokes.setup: The awkward moment of Elaine insulting his painting
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was intentionally bad as a psychological test
jokes.explanation: Nice recovery joke that explains the setup while commenting on Elaine's bluntness.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: You see, Miss Bates, that painting was intentionally bad.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: It's a psychological test. I wanted to see how you'd react to something objectively terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So you were testing me?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: Precisely. And you passed with flying colors. Your honest reaction shows a healthy, unfiltered personality.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I insulted your art.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: Yes, and that's exactly what I needed to know about you.

jokes.text: I think it's a toll call over there.
jokes.setup: Elaine is sitting far from the therapist's chair
jokes.punchline: Doctor's joke about the distance being so great it's long-distance
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about Elaine's resistance to therapy shown through physical distance.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: You're sitting quite far from my chair. At this rate, this will have to be a long-distance call.

jokes.text: This is going to be so easy for you, like an hour off.
jokes.setup: Elaine explains she's only there to prove she's fine
jokes.punchline: Thinks therapy will be effortless since she's healthy
jokes.explanation: Shows Elaine's confidence that she doesn't need help.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm only here to prove I'm fine. Therapy will be effortless since I'm healthy.

jokes.text: Nice note. I especially like the flower in the corner.
jokes.setup: Elaine presents a pre-written note declaring her mentally healthy
jokes.punchline: Doctor comments on the decorative touches she added
jokes.explanation: Shows Elaine's personality through the decorative note detail, revealing her need to make everything perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: Well, Elaine, this letter certainly declares you mentally healthy. But I have to say, the little hearts you drew around the border are a nice touch.

jokes.text: And if you leave, I'll have to read this article about what rats did at the University of Wisconsin.
jokes.setup: Psychiatrist explaining why he'll humor Elaine's request for questions
jokes.punchline: His boring alternative reading material makes Elaine preferable
jokes.explanation: Mild humor about the mundane nature of psychological research compared to actual human interaction.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: Well, you see, Elaine, I find you infinitely more interesting than my alternative reading material.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Really? What do you usually read?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: The phone book.

jokes.text: Talking.
jokes.setup: Doctor asks what radical therapy approach would work for her
jokes.punchline: Her simple, obvious answer deflates the psychological complexity
jokes.explanation: Elegant misdirection - the 'radical' therapy is just basic human communication, deflating pretentious psychology.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: What radical therapy approach would work for you?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: A date.

jokes.text: A sure sign of mental health, right? Right.
jokes.setup: Elaine claims to be very organized
jokes.punchline: Doctor's fake agreement reveals the opposite
jokes.explanation: Shows doctor's gentle way of handling patient's delusions.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm very organized. I have a system for everything.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: Oh yes, I can tell. Your apartment is immaculate.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Thank you! I'm glad someone notices.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Collins
dialogue.line: Well, I'm a psychiatrist. I'm trained to see things that aren't there.

jokes.text: So, uh, I guess I'll just have to give up the kids.
jokes.setup: Elaine explaining she can't give up gallery or taxi driving for financial reasons
jokes.punchline: Her logical but shocking conclusion about what to sacrifice
jokes.explanation: Dark humor perfectly capturing Elaine's overwhelmed state. The matter-of-fact delivery makes the shocking suggestion funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I can't give up the gallery, and I can't give up driving a cab. I need both incomes.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So I guess I'll have to give up eating.

jokes.text: Hey, listen, I'm just glad that one of us had the sense to stop before we did something that we would have both remembered fondly for the rest of our lives.
jokes.setup: Elaine thanks Alex for having the wisdom to avoid a romantic relationship
jokes.punchline: Alex's rueful acknowledgment that they missed out on something wonderful
jokes.explanation: Beautifully written line balancing humor with genuine emotion. Alex's self-deprecating wisdom about missed opportunities is both funny and touching.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Thank you for having the wisdom to avoid a romantic relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I guess I missed out on something wonderful.

jokes.text: I heard you are seeing a podiatrist.
jokes.setup: Latka trying to relate to Elaine's therapy
jokes.punchline: His confusion between psychiatrist and podiatrist
jokes.explanation: Classic Latka malapropism showing his innocent confusion with English medical terminology.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You go to psychiatrist? Is good, is good. My cousin Nicu, he go to podiatrist too.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: A podiatrist is a foot doctor, Latka.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, same thing! He sit in chair, he tell doctor his problems, doctor he fix the feet.

jokes.text: Because I told him I was having bad dreams, so he told me to wear looser shoes.
jokes.setup: Latka reveals he's also seeing a doctor for psychological help
jokes.punchline: The foot doctor's hilariously inappropriate advice for nightmares
jokes.explanation: Perfect absurdist logic - the complete mismatch between psychological problems and podiatric solutions is brilliantly Latka.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I go to see a psychologist about my nightmares.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A psychologist? That's good, Latka.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: No, no. Is not psychologist. Is podiatrist.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A podiatrist? What's a podiatrist gonna do about your nightmares?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: He say, 'Don't step on crack before going to bed.'

jokes.text: And you know, it worked. Boy, this is a great country.
jokes.setup: Latka's completely inappropriate medical advice from a foot doctor
jokes.punchline: His genuine enthusiasm for America's medical system despite the obvious misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Perfect Latka moment - his innocent immigrant optimism about America despite being completely confused. The fact that loose shoes 'worked' for nightmares is absurdly perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You know, in my country, we don't have foot doctors. But here in America, they have specialist for everything! Is wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: This doctor, he tell me, 'Latka, you have very bad bunion.' I say, 'No, no, I have very good life!' He say, 'No, is foot problem.' I say, 'How you know my life?'

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But you see, this is what I love about America. Only here they have man who spend his whole life looking at feet. Only in America!

tmdb_overview: Working two jobs and raising her kids takes its toll on Elaine, who seems to be losing her marbles.
insight: Nardo's deadpan unraveling carries a modest 74—character comedy over punchline setup.
